text: Hello, I'm Dr. Deepak Rao, consultant laparoscopic surgeon at the Motherhood Group of Hospitals at Bangalore. This is an unedited video of a total laparoscopic hysterectomy for a slightly bulky uterus with a patient who has had two previous caesarean sections.

The surgeon places the primary port supraumbilically, along with two left side ports and one right side contralateral port. The corneal structures are coagulated using a bipolar device and then cut with a shearer. The mesosalpingial vein region is visible, where the surgeon carefully desiccates the tissue before cutting.

The surgeon separates the corneal structures, revealing the round ligament, which is then fulgurated and cut. As the round ligament is completely divided, the broad ligament opens up, displaying its anterior and posterior leaves.
The surgeon applies additional cautery to the medial side of the corneal structure to control minor bleeding observed in the area.

The surgeon cleans the laparoscope lens by immersing it in warm saline and wiping it before reinserting it into the abdominal cavity. The video shows anterior bladder adhesions and the margins of the bladder, which are visible as the bladder fills up. A Foley's catheter is inserted to facilitate identification of the bladder margins.

The surgeon carefully identifies the bladder and uterine artery, dissecting anterior to the uterine artery below the level of previous caesarean incision adhesions, while creating a lateral window to avoid bladder injury.

Continuous traction is applied to the uterus to clearly identify the anatomical planes. The bladder is visibly filled, revealing its upper margin. The surgeon lifts the peritoneum to expose the space between the bladder and the cervix.

The surgeon dissects the bladder from adhesions to the uterus, revealing loose areolar tissue between the bladder and the anterior wall of the cervix. The bladder is lifted up, and the area is further dissected. Small venous oozing is visible, which may be coagulated or left alone.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum, revealing adhesions between the dome of the bladder and the anterior surface of the uterus. Suction is used to clear the area, while venous bleeding is managed by adjusting pneumoperitoneum pressure.

The surgeon cuts the adhesions close to the uterus, revealing the fat along the bladder wall. Dissection proceeds below this fat, reaching the level of the uterine artery on the opposite side. The peritoneum is opened and released, showing a clear plane between the bladder and uterus. Remaining fascia is dissected while staying anterior to the uterine arteries.

The surgeon bares the uterine vessels and prepares them for fulguration using a reusable Indian vessel sealing system. Posterior dissection of the peritoneum is performed up to the uterocycle, leaving loose areolar tissue around the vessels. The uterine artery and vein are clamped at three sites, ensuring safety as the ureter and bladder are distant from the fulguration site. Bipolar cautery is applied to desiccate internal tissue, followed by cutting perpendicular to the vessel and parallel to the cervical tissue. The anterior and posterior tissues, including the maconrodes and cardinal ligaments, are cut, and the descending cervical artery is visibly fulgurated.

The surgeon cuts the paracervical tissue, reaching the level of the vagina and releasing the posterior peritoneum to expose the posterior fornix. The myoma spiral is removed, and the entry point is fulgurated. The spiral is passed through the left upper port to lift the uterus, initiating the hysterectomy on the right side. The right caudal structures are manipulated, with the round ligament visible anteriorly, which is then bipolar cauterized and cut, opening the leaves of the broad ligament.

The surgeon uses a bipolar shearer connected to the cautery machine to seal and cut the corneal structures. The anterior and posterior leaves of the broad ligament are dissected, extending up to the utero-circular ligaments.

The surgeon cauterizes and cuts small vessels in the area, reaching the uterine vascular bundle on the right side. Loose fascial tissue is dissected to expose the vessels, while the bladder is dissected downwards. The vessel sealer is used from the right-hand side port, clamping the vessels at a ninety-degree angle in three locations for sealing.

The surgeon makes an initial cut, causing the pedicle of the uterine vascular bundle to fall away, and cauterizes the base of the pedicle to control bleeding. Bipolar forceps are used to manage oozing from the right corneal structures. A Babcock forceps is introduced from the left lower port to pick up and fulgurate the bleeding area with a Bessinger bipolar. The anterior vagina and fascia are clearly visible. The uterine artery is cut, and the pedicle is lateralized, revealing the uterine vein posteriorly, which is easily managed with a shearer due to the small size of the uterus.

The surgeon separates the uterosacral ligament and reaches the lateral vaginal fornix. Using gauze on an allus forceps controlled by the left hand, the vaginal fornices are elevated. The shearer is used to open the vault, cutting around the cervix and through the cervico-vaginal fascia, visibly separating the posterior vagina. Once half of the vagina is opened, the myoma screw is shifted to the right side.

The surgeon uses traction to address bleeding from the YMR screw entry point, cauterizing it with bipolar forceps to maintain a clean surgical field. The cervix, cervico-vaginal fascia, and bladder are clearly visible. A gauze piece is pushed towards the left side vaginal fornix, and the vagina is opened over the gauze. Once the vaginal opening is visualized, the gauze is withdrawn, and a mop is placed to prevent loss of pneumoperitoneum. A tooth grasper elevates the cervix, while the shearer cuts flush with the cervix to preserve vaginal length.
The surgeon completes the hysterectomy procedure, with the uterus fully removed from the pelvic cavity.
